title: You're absolutely welcome!

excerpt: For smaller projects, keeping things simple (like using redis.NewClient with default pooling) is totally reasonable , Go-Redis already…

content.html: You're absolutely welcome! You're spot-on , connection pooling is critical for high-throughput (high TPS) services, where efficient reuse of Redis connections dramatically reduces latency and resource overhead (e.g., avoiding repeated TCP handshakes and TLS setup).

For smaller projects, keeping things simple (like using redis.NewClient with default pooling) is totally reasonable , Go-Redis already includes a connection pool under the hood (default: PoolSize = 10 * runtime.NumCPU()), so even "basic" setups get decent pooling out of the box.
